category: stale meta description contradicting current page body

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty label values are supported by the page text. 'United States' is supported indirectly by the roaster location 'Brooklyn, NY' and US shipping/currency context. 'is_specialty_coffee' is supported by the page being a single-origin coffee product with detailed origin/process/variety/producer/altitude/harvest/tasting information. 'is_espresso: false' is supported because the page states 'Roast: Filter' and describes it as roasted on their filter profile, not espresso.

realism.reasons:
Overall this reads very plausibly like a real specialty coffee product page rendered to text. The structure is familiar: shop header, shipping banner, product title, price, bag size, stock/roast schedule, tasting notes, roast style, producer/origin/process/variety/altitude/harvest metadata, a short sourcing/cup-description paragraph, brew guidance, then variant data and related links. The coffee details are coherent and realistic: Guatemala from Huehuetenango, producer Jorge Vides at Finca La Bolsa, washed Bourbon/Caturra, 1300–1600 masl, and flavor notes like red apple/orange blossom/caramel all fit the category well. The pricing and variant ladder also look believable for a US roaster.

Minor tells keep it from a 5. The biggest is the meta description mismatch: it describes an Ethiopia Hambela Alaka natural with blueberry/lavender/milk chocolate, which clearly belongs to another product page and suggests synthetic assembly or sloppy templating. '250g whole bean' on-page while the meta says '12oz bags, roasted for espresso' is another inconsistency. The phrase '[default_for_inference]' inside the variant block feels machine-oriented rather than customer-facing extracted text. 'Roast: Filter' is plausible but slightly awkwardly labeled versus common wording like 'Roast level' or 'Best for filter.' Still, aside from those inconsistencies, the page is highly believable.

label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting notes: red apple, orange blossom, caramel

In the cup we find crisp red apple acidity, soft orange blossom aromatics and a long caramel sweetness.
label.coffee.producer_text: This fresh lot from Finca La Bolsa is the kind of clean, structured Guatemala we love to keep on the menu through late spring. Jorge Vides' team hand-sorted ripe cherry from the Bourbon and Caturra blocks above La Libertad, then depulped, fermented in clean water, washed and dried the parchment slowly on raised beds.
